items.title: worklog-opsdevnz
items.text:
Configurable CLI tool for creating and managing dated development worklogs.

Version: see pyproject.toml Status: Development License: Apache 2.0 Python: 3.14+

items.title: Overview
items.text:
Across multiple projects, teams kept reinventing the same pattern: a script that creates a dated Markdown file from a template, with YAML frontmatter for date, author, tags, and draft status. Each implementation had slightly different directory structures, section headers, and filenames — but the core concept was always the same.

worklog-opsdevnz formalises this pattern into a single reusable module. Install it once, configure it per project, and start logging.

items.title: Quick Start
items.text:
# Install (development)
uv pip install -e .

# Create today's worklog
worklog-opsdevnz create

# Create for a specific date with title
worklog-opsdevnz create -d 2026-04-28 -t "Module Scaffolding"

# List existing entries
worklog-opsdevnz list

# Scaffold a worklog.toml config
worklog-opsdevnz init

items.title: Configuration
items.text:
Each project gets a worklog.toml at its root:

worklog_dir = "docs/worklog"
structure = "flat"           # "flat", "year", or "year-month"
filename_pattern = "{date}-{slug}.md"
author = "Your Name"
default_tags = ["worklog", "log"]

[[sections]]
title = "🎯 Focus"

[[sections]]
title = "✅ Completed"

[[sections]]
title = "🧠 Notes"

[[sections]]
title = "⏳ Next"

items.title: Directory Structure
items.text:
The tool supports three directory layouts, configured per project:

Mode Layout Best For flat docs/worklog/2026-04-28-slug.md Occasional logging (< 20 entries) year docs/worklog/2026/2026-04-28-slug.md Moderate logging (default) year-month docs/worklog/2026/04/2026-04-28-slug.md Daily logging

items.title: Documentation
items.text:
• Specification — Functional requirements
  • Non-Functional Requirements — Code quality and design guidelines
  • User Stories — Persona-driven acceptance criteria
  • Design Decisions — Architecture choices and rationale

items.title: Scope
items.text:
In scope for 0.0.1 / 0.1.0: - Create, list, and init commands - Per-project configuration via worklog.toml - Three directory structure modes - Editor integration ($VISUAL / $EDITOR) - Template support (custom + retro templates)

Out of scope (deferred): - Zensical blog integration — see design decision - RSS feed generation - Auto-generated index pages (planned for 0.1.0) - Time tracking / timesheet aggregation

items.title: Development
items.text:
# Install with dev dependencies
uv pip install -e ".[dev]"

# Run tests
uv run pytest

# Lint
uv run ruff check src/

# Serve documentation
uv run zensical serve

This directory contains design decision records for worklog-opsdevnz.

Design decisions capture "how should we implement this?" before writing code. They link to spec requirements and preserve the thought process.

items.title: Process
items.text:
1. Proposal - Document the problem and proposed solution
  2. Decision - Record the chosen approach and rationale
  3. Implementation - Write the code
  4. Review - Verify implementation matches design
  5. Archive - Keep as decision record

items.title: Related
items.text:
• Specification - Functional requirements
  • User Stories - Persona-driven narratives

items.title: Design Decision: Directory Structure Modes
items.text: Status: Proposal Created: 2026-04-28 Spec Reference: FR-3

items.title: Problem
items.text: The existing prototype supports three directory structure modes: flat, year, and year-month. Which should be the default? Which should we recommend for new projects?

items.title: Analysis of Existing Usage
items.text: Project Current Structure Entry Count Notes outcome-engineering year/YYYY-MM-DD-worklog.md ~50 entries (2025-2026) Shell script, no config public/opsdev.nz flat/YYYY-MM-DD-slug.md 2 entries New, prototype config floydwilde.studio year-month-name/YYYY-MM-DD-worklog.md ~10 entries Hardcoded, month names

items.title: Flat (docs/worklog/2026-04-28-slug.md)
items.text:
Best for: Projects with < 20 entries, or projects that use a separate index page for navigation.

Pros: - Simplest structure - Easy to ls and grep - No nested directories to manage

Cons: - Doesn't scale — 100+ entries in one directory is unwieldy - File browsers and terminals become slow with many files - No natural archive boundaries

items.title: Year (docs/worklog/2026/2026-04-28-slug.md)
items.text:
Best for: Projects with moderate entry counts (20-100/year).

Pros: - Natural yearly archive boundaries - Keeps directory sizes manageable - Matches existing outcome-engineering pattern

Cons: - Still potentially large directories (daily logging = 365 files/year) - No monthly grouping for browsing

items.title: Year-Month (docs/worklog/2026/04/2026-04-28-slug.md)
items.text:
Best for: Projects with daily logging or high entry counts.

Pros: - Most granular — 10-30 entries per directory - Easy to browse archives by month - Scales indefinitely - Matches floydwilde.studio pattern (but with numeric months)

Cons: - Deeper directory nesting - Slightly more complex path resolution

items.title: Decision
items.text:
Default: year — Matches the most established pattern (outcome-engineering) and is a reasonable middle ground.

Recommended for daily logging: year-month — If a project expects to log most working days, this is the better choice.

Recommended for occasional logging: flat — If a project logs weekly or less frequently, flat is simplest.

The tool supports all three; the choice is per-project via worklog.toml.

items.title: Filename Pattern
items.text:
The default pattern is {date}-{slug}.md where: - {date} is the ISO date (2026-04-28) - {slug} is derived from the title suffix (lowercase, hyphenated)

This ensures: - Files sort chronologically when listed alphabetically - Dates are machine-parseable from filenames - Titles are human-readable in filenames

items.title: Related
items.text:
• specs/README.md FR-3 — Directory structure modes
  • worklog-as-blog.md — Zensical blog integration decision

items.title: Design Decision: Worklog as Blog via Zensical
items.text: Status: Decision — Blog integration deferred Created: 2026-04-28 Spec Reference: FR-1 (entry creation), FR-5 (listing)
path: Design Decision: Worklog as Blog via Zensical

items.title: Problem Statement
items.text:
Worklog entries are structurally similar to blog posts: dated, frontmatter-tagged Markdown files with an author and optional tags. Other teams using our worklog pattern have asked whether we should integrate with Zensical's blog feature to get chronological navigation, tag filtering, and RSS feeds.

The question: Should worklog-opsdevnz integrate with Zensical's blog system, or remain a standalone file-management tool?
path: Design Decision: Worklog as Blog via Zensical

items.title: Options Considered
path: Design Decision: Worklog as Blog via Zensical

items.title: Option A: Integrate with Zensical Blog
items.text:
Treat worklogs as a Zensical blog collection. Leverage Zensical's built-in chronological ordering, tag pages, and RSS generation.

Pros: - Chronological navigation out of the box - Tag filtering and author pages - RSS feed for worklog entries - Consistent with how blogs work in Zensical

Cons: - Zensical auto-navigation sorts by filename/folder alphabetically, not by date frontmatter — worklogs need date ordering - Flat structure doesn't scale (100+ entries in one directory) - Year or year-month structure breaks chronological display in auto-nav - Zensical has no documented mechanism for custom section labels with auto-navigation (we already hit this with dns-as-code → "Dns As Code") - Would couple worklog-opsdevnz to Zensical, reducing its usefulness as a standalone tool for non-Zensical projects - Requires investigation into Zensical plugin architecture or feature request - Time cost unknown — we have end-of-month beancount reconciliation and ledger security review taking priority
path: Design Decision: Worklog as Blog via Zensical

items.title: Option B: Standalone Tool + Auto-Generated Index
items.text:
Keep worklog-opsdevnz as a standalone CLI. Add an index subcommand that generates a reverse-chronological index.md from existing entries.

Pros: - No dependency on Zensical — works with any static site generator - index.md provides chronological listing regardless of directory structure - Can be enhanced later with Zensical integration if/when needed - Lower time cost — we already have the prototype, just need to package it - Other teams can adopt the tool without adopting Zensical

Cons: - No RSS feed (would need separate tool or Zensical blog integration later) - No tag filtering pages (would need Zensical or manual index generation) - Navigation in Zensical sidebar still alphabetical, not chronological
path: Design Decision: Worklog as Blog via Zensical

items.title: Option C: Hybrid — Standalone + Zensical-Compatible Output
items.text:
Build the standalone tool (Option B) but ensure the frontmatter and file structure are Zensical-blog-compatible. When a project wants blog features, they configure Zensical to treat the worklog directory as a blog collection.

Pros: - Best of both worlds: standalone now, blog-ready later - Zero coupling to Zensical in the core tool - Migration path is trivial: just add Zensical config

Cons: - Requires understanding Zensical's blog collection format - Still no chronological sidebar navigation without Zensical changes
path: Design Decision: Worklog as Blog via Zensical

items.title: Decision: Option C (Hybrid)
items.text: We will build worklog-opsdevnz as a standalone CLI tool (Option B) with Zensical-compatible frontmatter and structure (Option C). Blog integration is explicitly out of scope for 0.0.1 and 0.1.0.
path: Design Decision: Worklog as Blog via Zensical

items.title: Rationale
items.text:
1. Time constraint — End-of-month beancount reconciliation and ledger security review are the sprint priorities. Blog integration research is an unknown time sink.

  2. Standalone value first — The core problem is standardising the ad-hoc worklog scripts across three projects. That problem is solved by packaging the existing prototype. Blog features are a nice-to-have.

  3. No coupling — Other teams asking for this tool may not use Zensical. Keeping it standalone maximises adoption.

  4. Migration path is trivial — If we later decide to integrate with Zensical blog, the frontmatter format is already compatible. The only change would be adding Zensical-specific config to the consuming project.
path: Design Decision: Worklog as Blog via Zensical

items.title: What This Means for 0.0.1 / 0.1.0
items.text:
• In scope: Create, list, init commands. Config-driven templates. Three structure modes. Editor integration.
  • Out of scope: Zensical blog integration, RSS feeds, tag pages, chronological sidebar navigation.
  • Future consideration: worklog-opsdevnz generate-index subcommand that produces a reverse-chronological index.md with links, dates, and tags. This would solve the navigation problem without Zensical coupling.
path: Design Decision: Worklog as Blog via Zensical

items.title: Recommended Directory Structure
items.text:
For projects using Zensical, we recommend year-month structure:

docs/worklog/
├── index.md              # Auto-generated by worklog-opsdevnz (future)
├── 2026/
│   ├── 04/
│   │   ├── 2026-04-28-module-scaffolding.md
│   │   └── 2026-04-27-python-3-14-maintenance.md
│   └── 03/
│       └── 2026-03-28-worklog.md
└── 2025/
    └── 12/
        └── 2025-12-15-year-end-review.md

This keeps directories manageable (10-30 entries each) and enables archive browsing. The index.md (auto-generated) provides the chronological listing that Zensical auto-navigation cannot.
path: Design Decision: Worklog as Blog via Zensical

items.title: Zensical Limitations to Track
items.text: Limitation Impact Workaround Auto-nav sorts alphabetically, not by date Sidebar shows entries out of order Auto-generated index.md with correct ordering No custom section labels in auto-nav dns-as-code → "Dns As Code" Use explicit nav for section labels, auto for entries No glob patterns in nav config Can't auto-include all worklog files Auto-discovery mode (already supported)
path: Design Decision: Worklog as Blog via Zensical

items.title: UX Requirements (2026-05-22)
items.text:
From opsdev: the worklog should feel like a blog, not a file listing.

  1. No sidebar clutter — individual worklog entries must not appear as links in the Zensical navigation sidebar. Only the worklog index/landing page should appear.

  2. Browse by date — entries should be navigable chronologically (reverse chronological listing), with clear date grouping (year → month).

  3. Tag browsing — tags set in frontmatter should be filterable, allowing the reader to see all entries tagged opsdev or ramp or beancount, etc.

  4. Auto-generated index — a reverse-chronological index.md listing all entries with dates, titles, and tags. This is the primary navigation surface.

  5. Zensical blog integration (future) — if Zensical adds tag-filtering or chronological blog features, adopt them. Until then, the auto-generated index page is the navigation hub.
path: Design Decision: Worklog as Blog via Zensical

items.title: How Zensical handles this
items.text:
Zensical auto-navigation (the sidebar) picks up every .md file in the docs directory unless explicitly excluded. To avoid sidebar clutter from individual worklog entries, we need to explore:

  • Zensical's nav exclusion mechanism
  • Placing worklog entries outside the docs_dir
  • Using a plugin or custom template to hide them

This is tracked as a design investigation item.
path: Design Decision: Worklog as Blog via Zensical

items.title: Related
items.text:
• specs/README.md FR-1, FR-5 — Entry creation and listing
  • specs/NFR.md NFR-4 — Cross-platform compatibility
  • public/opsdev.nz/docs/worklog/index.md — Current worklog documentation
path: Design Decision: Worklog as Blog via Zensical

items.title: Functional Requirements Specification
items.text: Module: worklog-opsdevnz Module Version: see pyproject.toml Spec Last Updated: 2026-05-23 (from git history) Status: Draft

items.title: Versioning Model
items.text:
This spec follows the OpsDev.nz versioning model:

Field Source Meaning Module Version pyproject.toml Version of the module being specified Spec Last Updated Git history When this spec was last modified

Use specmaint sync-metadata to sync both fields from their sources.

items.title: Related Documents
items.text:
• Non-Functional Requirements - Code quality, security, and design guidelines
  • Design Decisions - Architecture and integration decisions
  • User Stories - Persona-driven narratives

items.title: FR-1.1: Basic Creation
items.text:
• FR-1.1.1: Tool MUST create a new Markdown file with YAML frontmatter containing title, date, author, tags, and draft fields
  • FR-1.1.2: Tool MUST default to today's date when no -p / --previous flag provided
  • FR-1.1.3: Tool MUST use -p / --previous to open yesterday's worklog
  • FR-1.1.4: Tool MUST NOT overwrite existing entries — if a file already exists for the given date, it MUST open the existing file instead

items.title: FR-1.1.A: Frontmatter Format
items.text:
• FR-1.1.A.1: Frontmatter MUST use YAML format delimited by --- markers
  • FR-1.1.A.2: title field MUST follow the pattern "Work Log - {date}"
  • FR-1.1.A.3: date field MUST be ISO 8601 format (YYYY-MM-DD)
  • FR-1.1.A.4: author field MUST come from worklog.toml config
  • FR-1.1.A.5: tags field MUST be a YAML list from default_tags in config
  • FR-1.1.A.6: draft field MUST default to false

items.title: FR-1.1.B: Body Generation
items.text:
• FR-1.1.B.1: Tool MUST generate section headers from the sections list in config
  • FR-1.1.B.2: Each section MUST be an H2 heading (## Title)
  • FR-1.1.B.3: Sections MUST appear in the order defined in config
  • FR-1.1.B.4: A blank line MUST separate frontmatter from the first section heading

items.title: FR-2.1: Config Discovery
items.text:
• FR-2.1.1: Tool MUST search for worklog.toml, worklog.yaml, or worklog.yml starting from the current working directory and walking up to the filesystem root
  • FR-2.1.2: Tool MUST use the first config file found
  • FR-2.1.3: If no config file is found, tool MUST use built-in defaults

items.title: FR-2.2: Configuration Schema
items.text:
The worklog.toml file supports the following fields:

Field Type Required Default Description worklog_dir string No "docs/worklog" Base directory for worklog files structure string No "year" Directory structure: "flat", "year", "year-month" filename_pattern string No "{day}-{month}-{year}-{suffix}.md" Filename template. Default: 23-05-2026-worklog.md filename_suffix string No "worklog" Configurable suffix appended to the filename author string No $USER or "unknown" Default author name default_tags list No ["internal", "log"] Default YAML tags sections list No (see defaults) Ordered section headers

items.title: FR-2.3: Config Initialisation
items.text:
• FR-2.3.1: worklog-opsdevnz init MUST create a worklog.toml in the current directory
  • FR-2.3.2: Tool MUST NOT overwrite an existing config file
  • FR-2.3.3: Generated config MUST include all default fields with commented explanations

items.title: FR-3.1: Flat Mode (structure = "flat")
items.text:
• FR-3.1.1: All worklog files placed directly in worklog_dir
  • FR-3.1.2: Filename: YYYY-MM-DD-{suffix}.md (year first to sort correctly)

items.title: FR-3.2: Year Mode (structure = "year") — default
items.text:
• FR-3.2.1: Files placed in worklog_dir/YYYY/
  • FR-3.2.2: Filename: DD-MM-YYYY-{suffix}.md
  • FR-3.2.3: Example: docs/worklog/2026/23-05-2026-worklog.md

items.title: FR-3.3: Year-Month Mode (structure = "year-month")
items.text:
• FR-3.3.1: Files placed in worklog_dir/YYYY/MM/
  • FR-3.3.2: Filename: DD-MM-YYYY-{suffix}.md
  • FR-3.3.3: Example: docs/worklog/2026/05/23-05-2026-worklog.md

items.title: FR-4.1: Editor Resolution
items.text:
• FR-4.1.1: Tool MUST check for editor in this order:
  • -e / --editor CLI argument
  • $VISUAL environment variable
  • $EDITOR environment variable
  • FR-4.1.2: If no editor is configured, tool MUST print the file path and exit cleanly
  • FR-4.1.3: Tool MUST resolve the editor command via shutil.which() and report if not found in PATH

items.title: FR-4.2: File Opening
items.text:
• FR-4.2.1: Tool MUST invoke the editor with the worklog file path as argument
  • FR-4.2.2: Tool MUST wait for the editor process to complete before exiting

items.title: FR-5.1: List Command
items.text:
• FR-5.1.1: worklog-opsdevnz list MUST find all .md files under worklog_dir
  • FR-5.1.2: Tool MUST exclude index.md, template.md, and retro-template.md from listing
  • FR-5.1.3: Entries MUST be sorted by date (newest first)
  • FR-5.1.4: Output MUST show the relative path from worklog_dir

items.title: FR-5.2: List Output (0.1.0+)
items.text:
• FR-5.2.1: Tool SHOULD parse frontmatter to display title, date, and tags
  • FR-5.2.2: Output SHOULD use Rich for formatted table display

items.title: FR-6.1: Command
items.text: worklog-opsdevnz — create or open today's worklog (default, no args required). list and init subcommands are planned for future versions.

items.title: FR-6.2: Global Options
items.text: Option Description -p, --previous Open yesterday's worklog instead of today's -e, --editor Override editor command for this run

items.title: FR-6.3: Error Handling
items.text:
• FR-6.3.1: Invalid date format MUST produce a clear error message and non-zero exit
  • FR-6.3.2: Missing worklog_dir MUST produce a clear error message
  • FR-6.3.3: All errors MUST write to stderr

items.title: FR-7.1: Custom Templates
items.text:
• FR-7.1.1: Tool MUST support a template field in config pointing to a Markdown file
  • FR-7.1.2: Template files MUST support {{DATE}} and {{TITLE}} placeholders
  • FR-7.1.3: If no template specified, tool MUST use the built-in default template

items.title: FR-7.2: Retro Templates
items.text:
• FR-7.2.1: Tool MUST support a separate retro_template field in config
  • FR-7.2.2: worklog-opsdevnz create --retro MUST use the retro template

items.title: 0.0.1 Staging Complete When:
items.text:
• Module directory structure created under modules/worklog_opsdevnz/
  • pyproject.toml with correct metadata and dependencies
  • Functional and non-functional requirements documented
  • Design decision documented for Zensical blog integration (deferred)
  • Prototype scripts/worklog.py from opsdev.nz reviewed and mapped to requirements
  • Empty src/worklog_opsdevnz/ package scaffolded
  • Empty tests/ directory scaffolded

items.title: 0.0.2 MVP Complete When:
items.text:
• worklog-opsdevnz (no args) creates today's worklog with YAML frontmatter and config-driven sections
  • -p / --previous opens yesterday's worklog
  • -e / --editor overrides the configured editor
  • Config discovery walks up the tree, falls back to defaults
  • Three structure modes work: flat, year, year-month
  • Filename follows DD-MM-YYYY-{suffix}.md (year/month modes) or YYYY-MM-DD-{suffix}.md (flat)
  • Tests cover config loading, path resolution, frontmatter generation

items.title: 0.1.0 MVP Complete When:
items.text:
• create command works with all three structure modes
  • list command sorts by date and shows frontmatter metadata
  • init command generates valid worklog.toml
  • Editor integration works with $VISUAL / $EDITOR
  • Template support (custom + retro) implemented
  • Tests cover config loading, path resolution, frontmatter generation
  • Published to PyPI as worklog-opsdevnz

items.title: v0.0.1 (staging) — Complete
items.text:
• Module scaffolded with template
  • pyproject.toml with metadata and dependencies
  • Specs, stories, and design docs in place
  • GitHub Actions CI and Dependabot configured

items.text:

Document: specs/README.md Module: worklog-opsdevnz Version: see pyproject.toml

items.title: Non-Functional Requirements
items.text:
Module: worklog-opsdevnz Spec Last Updated: 2026-04-28 (from git history) Status: Draft Created: 2026-04-28

The "Spec Last Updated" date is auto-generated from git history by specmaint sync-metadata.

items.title: NFR-1.1: No Hardcoded Project Assumptions
items.text:
Requirement: The tool MUST NOT assume any particular project structure, section headers, or directory layout beyond what is specified in worklog.toml.

Rationale: - Different projects have different worklog needs (opsdev.nz vs floydwilde.studio vs private ledgers) - Hardcoded assumptions create maintenance burden and reduce reusability - Configuration-driven approach enables any project to adopt the tool

Correct Approach:

# Each project defines its own sections
[[sections]]
title = "🎯 Focus"

[[sections]]
title = "✅ Completed"

# GOOD: Config-driven
sections = config.get("sections", DEFAULT_SECTIONS)

items.title: NFR-2.1: Type Hints
items.text:
Requirement: All public functions MUST have type hints.

def resolve_path(config: dict[str, Any], iso_date: str, title_suffix: str | None = None) -> Path:

items.title: NFR-2.2: Linting
items.text: Requirement: Code MUST pass ruff linting with the project's configured rules.

items.title: NFR-2.3: CLI Framework
items.text:
Requirement: The CLI MUST use Click (not argparse) for consistency with other OpsDev.nz modules (specmaint, yalh, sprintmaint).

Rationale: - Click provides subcommand groups, better help text, and consistent UX - All other OpsDev.nz CLI tools use Click - Reduces cognitive load for users familiar with the ecosystem

items.title: NFR-3.1: Relative to Project Root
items.text:
Requirement: The worklog_dir in config is always relative to the directory containing the worklog.toml file, NOT the current working directory.

Rationale: - Users may run the command from a subdirectory (e.g., cd docs/ && worklog-opsdevnz create) - The config file location anchors the path resolution

items.title: NFR-3.2: Directory Creation
items.text: Requirement: Tool MUST create intermediate directories as needed (mkdir -p behaviour).

items.title: NFR-4.1: Python Version
items.text: Requirement: Tool MUST support Python 3.14+.

items.title: NFR-4.2: Cross-Platform
items.text: Requirement: Tool MUST work on Linux and macOS. Windows support is not a 0.0.1 priority but the code SHOULD NOT actively break on Windows.

items.title: NFR-4.3: Config Format
items.text: Requirement: Tool MUST support TOML as the primary config format. YAML support is optional (via pyyaml dependency).

items.title: NFR-5.1: CLI Help
items.text: Requirement: All commands and options MUST have descriptive help text accessible via --help.

items.title: NFR-5.2: Examples
items.text: Requirement: The --help output for create MUST include usage examples.

items.title: NFR-5.3: Changelog
items.text: Requirement: All changes MUST be documented in CHANGELOG.md following the Keep a Changelog format.

items.title: NFR-6.1: Unit Tests
items.text: Requirement: Core functionality (config loading, path resolution, frontmatter generation) MUST have unit tests.

items.title: NFR-6.2: No Filesystem Side Effects in Tests
items.text: Requirement: Tests MUST use tmp_path (pytest) or equivalent to avoid modifying real files.

items.title: NFR-6.3: No Network Dependencies
items.text: Requirement: Tests MUST NOT require network access.

items.title: NFR-7.1: Backward Compatibility
items.text:
Requirement: The module MUST be able to read worklog entries created by the existing scripts/worklog.py in opsdev.nz without modification.

Rationale: Existing worklog entries in public/opsdev.nz/docs/worklog/ and docs/worklog/ must remain valid.

items.title: NFR-7.2: Config Compatibility
items.text: Requirement: The module MUST accept the same worklog.toml format as the prototype, with no breaking changes to existing config files.

items.title: Compliance Checklist
items.text:
Requirement Status Notes No hardcoded project assumptions 📋 Planned Will be enforced in implementation Type hints on public functions 📋 Planned mypy configured Click CLI framework 📋 Planned Dependency declared Relative path resolution 📋 Planned Will use config dir as anchor Python 3.14+ 📋 Planned requires-python set Cross-platform 📋 Planned pathlib for all paths TOML config primary 📋 Planned tomllib (stdlib 3.14) Unit tests 📋 Planned pytest configured Backward compatible with prototype 📋 Planned Same config schema

items.title: User Stories
items.text: This directory contains user stories for worklog-opsdevnz, organised by persona and priority.

items.title: Story 1: Daily Worklog Entry
items.text:
As a developer working on a project, I want to create a dated worklog entry with my project's section headers, so that I can quickly capture what I did today without thinking about formatting.

Acceptance Criteria: - [ ] Running worklog-opsdevnz create creates a file with today's date - [ ] File contains YAML frontmatter with title, date, author, tags, draft - [ ] File contains section headers from worklog.toml - [ ] File opens in my configured editor - [ ] Running again on the same day opens the existing file

items.title: Story 2: Per-Project Configuration
items.text:
As a developer working on multiple projects, I want each project to have its own worklog template and directory structure, so that my worklogs match each project's conventions without manual setup.

Acceptance Criteria: - [ ] Each project has its own worklog.toml - [ ] Tool finds config by walking up from current directory - [ ] Different projects can use different structure modes (flat, year, year-month) - [ ] Different projects can have different section headers

items.title: Story 3: Browse Existing Entries
items.text:
As a developer returning to a project after a break, I want to list my recent worklog entries, so that I can quickly find what I was working on last.

Acceptance Criteria: - [ ] worklog-opsdevnz list shows all entries sorted newest-first - [ ] Output shows relative paths from the worklog directory - [ ] Excludes index.md and template files from listing

items.title: Story 4: Standardise Across Projects
items.text:
As an opsdev engineer maintaining multiple codebases, I want to install one tool that works across all my projects, so that I don't need to maintain separate worklog scripts for each.

Acceptance Criteria: - [ ] Tool installable via pip install worklog-opsdevnz - [ ] Works with existing worklog.toml format from prototype - [ ] Compatible with entries created by the old scripts/worklog.py - [ ] CLI uses Click for consistency with other OpsDev.nz tools

items.title: Story 5: Retrospective Entries (0.1.0)
items.text:
As a developer who runs retrospectives, I want to create a worklog entry from a retro template, so that my retros have a consistent structure different from daily logs.

Acceptance Criteria: - [ ] worklog-opsdevnz create --retro uses the retro template - [ ] Retro template is configurable per project - [ ] Retro entries are distinguishable from daily logs (different filename or tag)

items.title: Story 6: Auto-Generated Index (0.1.0)
items.text:
As a reader of worklogs on a documentation site, I want an index page that lists entries in reverse-chronological order, so that I can browse worklogs without relying on alphabetical sidebar navigation.

Acceptance Criteria: - [ ] worklog-opsdevnz generate-index creates/updates index.md - [ ] Index lists entries newest-first with title, date, and tags - [ ] Index is Markdown format compatible with Zensical/MkDocs

items.title: Priority Order
items.text: Story Priority Version Target Story 1: Daily entry High 0.0.1 / 0.1.0 Story 2: Per-project config High 0.0.1 / 0.1.0 Story 3: Browse entries High 0.0.1 / 0.1.0 Story 4: Standardise High 0.0.1 / 0.1.0 Story 5: Retro entries Medium 0.1.0 Story 6: Auto-generated index Medium 0.1.0
